---
title: "Coordinates and Midpoints"
jupyter: python3
---

tikzfigure can compute new node positions geometrically from existing ones.
`fig.midpoint(node_a, node_b)` places a new node exactly halfway between two
existing nodes.  For a lighter alternative with no visible output, use
`fig.add_coordinate(label, x, y)` to define a named invisible waypoint.

This tutorial covers:
- basic midpoint usage
- using label strings as references
- edge labels on a triangle
- recursive bisection
- computing a triangle's centroid via iterated midpoints
- named coordinates as invisible path waypoints
- routing paths via intermediate coordinates
- expression-based coordinates with the `calc` library

```{python}
from tikzfigure import TikzFigure
```


```{python}
fig = TikzFigure()

A = fig.add_node(
    0, 0, label="A", content="$A$", shape="circle", fill="blue!20", draw="blue"
)
B = fig.add_node(
    6, 0, label="B", content="$B$", shape="circle", fill="blue!20", draw="blue"
)
C = fig.add_node(
    2, 4, label="C", content="$C$", shape="circle", fill="blue!20", draw="blue"
)

fig.draw([A.center, "B", "C"],
    center=True, cycle=True, color="blue", fill="blue!10", line_width="1.5pt")

fig.show()
```


## Basic midpoint

Pass two nodes (or label strings) and any additional node kwargs.
The returned node has `.x` and `.y` set to the computed midpoint.

```{python}
fig = TikzFigure()

n1 = fig.add_node(
    0, 0, label="A", content="A", shape="circle", fill="blue!30", draw="blue"
)
n2 = fig.add_node(
    4, 0, label="B", content="B", shape="circle", fill="red!30", draw="red"
)

mid = fig.midpoint(
    n1, "B", label="M", content="M", shape="circle", fill="green!30", draw="green"
)

fig.draw([n1, n2], options=["dashed", "gray"])

fig.show()
```

{{< include includes/tikz_code_callout.qmd >}}

## Using label strings

You can refer to any node by its label string instead of keeping the Python object.

```{python}
fig = TikzFigure()

fig.add_node(
    0, 2, label="P", content="P", shape="circle", fill="orange!40", draw="orange"
)
fig.add_node(
    6, 2, label="Q", content="Q", shape="circle", fill="orange!40", draw="orange"
)

fig.midpoint(
    "P", "Q", label="PQ", content="mid", shape="circle", fill="white", draw="black"
)

fig.draw(["P", "Q"], options=["dashed", "gray"])

fig.show()
```

{{< include includes/tikz_code_callout.qmd >}}

## Edge labels on a triangle

A classic use case: place a label at the midpoint of each edge.

```{python}
fig = TikzFigure(figure_setup="every node/.append style={draw, minimum size=0.7cm}")

fig.add_node(0, 0, label="A", content="A", shape="circle", fill="blue!20")
fig.add_node(4, 0, label="B", content="B", shape="circle", fill="blue!20")
fig.add_node(2, 3, label="C", content="C", shape="circle", fill="blue!20")

fig.draw(["A", "B"], color="black")
fig.draw(["B", "C"], color="black")
fig.draw(["A", "C"], color="black")

fig.midpoint("A", "B", label="AB", content="$c$", draw="none", fill="yellow!60")
fig.midpoint("B", "C", label="BC", content="$a$", draw="none", fill="yellow!60")
fig.midpoint("A", "C", label="AC", content="$b$", draw="none", fill="yellow!60")

fig.show()
```

{{< include includes/tikz_code_callout.qmd >}}

## Recursive bisection

Repeatedly calling `midpoint` bisects a segment.
Here we produce three levels of binary subdivision.

```{python}
fig = TikzFigure()

dot = dict(
    shape="circle",
    minimum_size="6pt",
    fill="black",
    draw="black",
    inner_sep="0pt",
    content="",
)

left = fig.add_node(0, 0, label="L", **dot)
right = fig.add_node(8, 0, label="R", **dot)
fig.draw([left, right], color="gray")

m1 = fig.midpoint(left, right, label="M1", **dot)
m2a = fig.midpoint(left, m1, label="M2a", **dot)
m2b = fig.midpoint(m1, right, label="M2b", **dot)

fig.midpoint(left, m2a, label="M3a", **dot)
fig.midpoint(m2a, m1, label="M3b", **dot)
fig.midpoint(m1, m2b, label="M3c", **dot)
fig.midpoint(m2b, right, label="M3d", **dot)

fig.show()
```

{{< include includes/tikz_code_callout.qmd >}}

## Triangle centroid via iterated midpoints

The centroid $G$ of a triangle satisfies $G = \frac{A+B+C}{3}$.
We can reach it with two midpoint calls:
$G = \text{midpoint}(\text{midpoint}(A, B),\; C)$

(This is equivalent to taking 2/3 along the median from $C$ to the midpoint of $AB$.)

```{python}
fig = TikzFigure()

A = fig.add_node(
    0, 0, label="A", content="$A$", shape="circle", fill="blue!20", draw="blue"
)
B = fig.add_node(
    6, 0, label="B", content="$B$", shape="circle", fill="blue!20", draw="blue"
)
C = fig.add_node(
    2, 4, label="C", content="$C$", shape="circle", fill="blue!20", draw="blue"
)

fig.draw([A.center, "B", "C"],
    center=True, cycle=True, color="blue", fill="blue!10", line_width="1.5pt")

# Midpoint of BC - used to draw the median from A
mid_BC = fig.midpoint(
    B,
    C,
    label="MBC",
    content="",
    shape="circle",
    minimum_size="4pt",
    fill="gray",
    draw="gray",
    inner_sep="0pt",
)
fig.draw([A, mid_BC], options=["dashed"], color="gray")

# Centroid = midpoint(midpoint(A, B), C)
centroid = fig.midpoint(
    fig.midpoint(A, B, label="_MAB", content=""),
    C,
    label="G",
    content="$G$",
    shape="circle",
    fill="red!60",
    draw="red",
)

fig.show()
```

{{< include includes/tikz_code_callout.qmd >}}

## Named coordinates as path waypoints

`fig.add_coordinate(label, x, y)` plants an invisible named point — no box,
no content, no border.  It emits `\coordinate (label) at (x,y);` in TikZ and
can be referenced in any path list just like a node label.

This is lighter than `add_node` when you only need a reusable geometric
anchor, not a visible element.

```{python}
fig = TikzFigure()

# Four invisible corners of a diamond
fig.add_coordinate("top",   x=3, y=4)
fig.add_coordinate("right", x=6, y=2)
fig.add_coordinate("bot",   x=3, y=0)
fig.add_coordinate("left",  x=0, y=2)

# Draw a diamond outline and its diagonals
fig.draw(["top", "right", "bot", "left"], cycle=True,
         color="teal", line_width=2)
fig.draw(["top", "bot"], options=["dashed"], color="gray")
fig.draw(["left", "right"], options=["dashed"], color="gray")

# A visible centre node for reference
fig.add_node(3, 2, content="centre", draw="none", font=r"\small")

fig.show()
```

{{< include includes/tikz_code_callout.qmd >}}

## Routing paths via intermediate coordinates

A common TikZ pattern is bending a connection around an obstacle by routing
it through an invisible corner coordinate.  Here two nodes are connected with
an L-shaped arrow: the path goes horizontally to a corner, then vertically up.

```{python}
fig = TikzFigure()

fig.add_node(0, 0, label="src", shape="rectangle",
             draw="blue!70", fill="blue!15",
             minimum_width="1.8cm", minimum_height="0.8cm", content="source")
fig.add_node(5, 3, label="dst", shape="rectangle",
             draw="orange!80", fill="orange!15",
             minimum_width="1.8cm", minimum_height="0.8cm", content="dest")

# Corner at the same column as dst, same row as src
fig.add_coordinate("corner", x=5, y=0)

fig.draw(["src.east", "corner", "dst.south"],
         options=["->", "thick"], color="purple",
         segment_options=[
             {"connector": "--"},   # horizontal segment: straight line
             {"connector": "--"},   # vertical segment: straight line
         ])

fig.show()
```

{{< include includes/tikz_code_callout.qmd >}}

## Coordinates from expressions (`calc` library)

When you add `\usetikzlibrary{calc}` via `document_setup`, the `at` parameter
of `add_coordinate` accepts full calc expressions.  The `$(A)!t!(B)$` syntax
places a point a fraction `t` of the way from `A` to `B`.

```{python}
fig = TikzFigure(document_setup=r"\usetikzlibrary{calc}")

fig.add_node(0, 0, label="A", shape="circle",
             fill="blue!20", draw="blue!70",
             minimum_size="1cm", content="$A$")
fig.add_node(8, 0, label="B", shape="circle",
             fill="red!20", draw="red!70",
             minimum_size="1cm", content="$B$")
fig.add_node(4, 5, label="C", shape="circle",
             fill="green!20", draw="green!70",
             minimum_size="1cm", content="$C$")

# Invisible midpoints of each edge using calc expressions
fig.add_coordinate("mAB", at="$(A)!0.5!(B)$")
fig.add_coordinate("mBC", at="$(B)!0.5!(C)$")
fig.add_coordinate("mCA", at="$(C)!0.5!(A)$")

# Outline triangle
fig.draw(["A.center", "B.center", "C.center"], cycle=True,
         color="gray!50", line_width=1)

# Medial triangle connecting the three edge midpoints
fig.draw(["mAB", "mBC", "mCA"], cycle=True,
         color="purple", line_width=2, fill="purple!10", fill_opacity=0.5)

fig.show()
```

{{< include includes/tikz_code_callout.qmd >}}
